questions.question: Explain the need for environmental legislation in India.
keyPoints: Protection of natural resources from exploitation
Prevention and control of pollution
Conservation of biodiversity and forests
Sustainable development for future generations
Legal framework for enforcing environmental standards

questions.question: What is Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) under E-waste Management Rules?
keyPoints: Policy approach making producers responsible for end-of-life management of products
Producers must register and comply with collection and recycling targets
Encourages design for environment and recycling
Reduces burden on municipal waste management systems
Implementation through producer responsibility organizations (PROs)

questions.question: Write a short note on the Montreal Protocol.
keyPoints: International treaty adopted in 1987
Aims to protect the ozone layer
Phases out production of ozone-depleting substances (CFCs, HCFCs)
India ratified the protocol in 1991
One of the most successful international environmental agreements

questions.question: List four hazardous substances found in E-waste and their effects.
keyPoints: Lead: Causes neurological damage, kidney problems
Mercury: Damages brain and nervous system
Cadmium: Causes kidney damage and bone demineralization
Chromium: Can cause allergic reactions and lung cancer
Brominated flame retardants: Endocrine disruption

questions.question: Explain Article 51A(g) of the Indian Constitution.
keyPoints: Fundamental Duty added by 42nd Amendment (1976)
Every citizen shall protect and improve the natural environment
Includes protection of forests, lakes, rivers, and wildlife
Duty to have compassion for living creatures
Complements Article 48A (Directive Principle)

questions.question: What are the functions of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)?
keyPoints: Advises Central Government on environmental matters
Coordinates with State Pollution Control Boards
Sets standards for emission and discharge of pollutants
Monitors air and water quality nationally
Collects and disseminates information on pollution

questions.question: Discuss the major environmental legislation in India and their objectives.
keyPoints: Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974: Prevent water pollution, establish pollution control boards
Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981: Control air pollution, establish emission standards
Environmental Protection Act, 1986: Umbrella legislation for environmental protection
Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980: Conserve forest land, prevent deforestation
Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972: Protect wildlife, establish protected areas
Objectives: Sustainable development, pollution control, biodiversity conservation, legal enforcement mechanism

questions.question: Explain the E-waste (Management) Rules, 2016 and discuss its key features.
keyPoints: Rules notified under Environment Protection Act, 1986
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework introduced
Producers must register and meet collection/recycling targets
Role of Producer Responsibility Organizations (PROs) defined
Inventory management through online portal
Safety and health requirements for handling e-waste
Penalties for non-compliance
Goal: Proper collection, storage, transport, and recycling of e-waste

questions.question: Discuss the concept of Sustainable Development and its relevance to environmental legislation.
keyPoints: Definition: Development meeting present needs without compromising future generations
Brundtland Commission (1987) popularized the concept
Three pillars: Economic growth, Social equity, Environmental protection
Relevance to legislation: Provides guiding principle for laws
Indian laws incorporate sustainable development principles
Need for balancing industrial growth with environmental protection
International agreements promote sustainable practices
Role of technology and green computing in achieving sustainability

questions.question: Explain the structure and functions of pollution control boards in India.
keyPoints: Two-tier structure: CPCB at center, SPCBs in states
CPCB functions: Advise government, set standards, coordinate with states, monitor national pollution
SPCB functions: Implement laws at state level, grant consents, monitor compliance, enforce regulations
Powers: Entry and inspection, sample collection, analysis, prosecution
Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981 established these boards
Penalties for non-compliance: Closure, prosecution, fines
Role in industrial licensing and environmental clearance
